title: Dog Black Crusty Ear Discharge: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Find out why your dog has black crusty ear discharge. Learn about yeast infections, ear mites, and when to book a non-emergency vet appointment.
content_html:
What is Black Crusty Ear Discharge?

Black or dark brown crusty discharge in a dog's ear is a common sign of an underlying issue, most frequently associated with yeast infections (Malassezia) or ear mites. If the discharge looks like coffee grounds, it is often a hallmark sign of mites. If it is waxy, dark, and has a pungent, musty odor, a yeast or bacterial overgrowth is the likely culprit. This debris is a mix of wax, inflammatory byproducts, and the infecting organisms themselves.

Is This an Emergency?

Urgency Level: Low. While black ear discharge is uncomfortable and can lead to a more severe infection if left untreated, it is rarely a medical emergency. You should schedule a standard veterinary appointment within the next few days. However, if your dog is tilting their head significantly, losing their balance, or showing extreme pain, you should seek care sooner as these can be signs of a ruptured eardrum or inner ear involvement.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of the ear canal and the discharge on a cotton ball can be incredibly helpful for your veterinary team. A photo allows a professional to assess the level of inflammation (redness and swelling) and the characteristics of the discharge before you even arrive. This helps the triage team determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen and helps the vet prepare the necessary diagnostic tests, such as an ear cytology.

faqs.question: Can I use hydrogen peroxide to clean my dog's ears?
faqs.answer: No, you should avoid using hydrogen peroxide as it can be irritating to the sensitive skin of the ear canal and may cause further inflammation. Use a vet-approved ear cleaning solution instead.

faqs.question: Are ear mites contagious to other pets?
faqs.answer: Yes, ear mites are highly contagious between dogs and cats. If one pet is diagnosed, all pets in the household usually need to be treated.

faqs.question: How do vets diagnose the cause of the discharge?
faqs.answer: Vets typically perform an ear cytology, where they take a swab of the discharge and look at it under a microscope to identify yeast, bacteria, or mites.
